messages.content:
A statistician walks into a room and wants to
know how likely it is that someone in the room shares a birthday with HER
specifically (not with each other — with her).

Assume 365 equally likely birthdays, ignore leap years, and that she knows
her own birthday. What is the minimum number of OTHER people that must be in
the room so that the probability at least one of them shares HER birthday
exceeds 50%?

Think carefully about exactly which event is being asked about.


When you are finished, output your final answer on its own line, as the last line of your response, in the exact format:
ANSWER: <your answer>

text:
This is the "same birthday as you" variant, not the classic birthday paradox. Each of the n other people independently misses her birthday with probability 364/365, so:

P(at least one match) = 1 − (364/365)ⁿ > 0.5
⇒ (364/365)ⁿ < 0.5
⇒ n > ln 2 / ln(365/364) ≈ 0.693147 / 0.0027435 ≈ 252.65

So n = 253 is the minimum. Check: 1 − (364/365)²⁵² ≈ 0.4995 (not enough), while 1 − (364/365)²⁵³ ≈ 0.5005 (just over 50%).

(Note this is much larger than the famous 23 from the classic problem, which asks about *any* pair matching — here the match must be with her specific birthday.)

ANSWER: 253
